data.title: Align Technology: Only International Growth Can Save Them Now (Downgrade)
data.description:
Based in Tempe, AZ, and founded in 1997, Align Technology, Inc. (ALGN) is a leading manufacturer of orthodontic treatments globally. They sell clear aligners under the Invisalign and Vivera brands as well as iTero scanners, alongside its associated CAD software, Exocad. Invisalign, now a well-known if not household name, is “the #1 doctor recommended clear aligner brand in the world”, based on a 2022 survey. Since launching in 1999, they have been used by 22.1 million people worldwide. The company has retained relationships with over 2 million orthodontists globally – almost 300,000 of which are presently operating and using Align’s products – which continues to sustain their leading market share and control of large parts of the supply chain. This includes the physicians who prescribe treatments after scanning patient’s teeth using Align’s own hardware and software, the supply of orthodontic solutions themselves, alongside the manufacturers that build the aligners for patient’s teeth. Alongside their strong brand and network of ongoing relationships with physicians, this provides them with a competitive advantage over their smaller competitors. That said, competitive pressured have increased, which has brought into question their moat based around barriers to entry for competitors.

Shares have underperformed the S&P500 over the last decade, and particularly poorly since their peak in 2021, during the Covid bull run. However, since my last coverage (a “buy” rating) in August last year, shares have gained around 30%. I seemed to have called the bottom on the stock – or close to it – which is probably more luck than ability on my part, as I believed their fundamentals always pointed to a decent rebound. The questions were when and how low would the stock go? I would encourage readers to return to my first coverage as it provides something of a deep dive on the company and what I perceived at the time to be its long-term durable competitive advantages amidst market and industry dynamics.

In that article, I highlighted structural growth in the industry, which Align was sure to benefit from, being the undisputed market leader. However, I noted that the growth story had ground to a halt. Revenue has been flat for about 4 years, with margins slightly declining due to a slight uptick in operating costs.

EPS has grown slowly – from $4.05 on a TTM basis in Q1 2023, to $5.66 in Q4 2025 – but has been mainly flat over the last few years with the slight uptick due to aggressive stock buybacks. Below shows their net common stock issued/repurchased, which offsets dilution from stock-based compensation. We can see that shareholders have been on the receiving end here, which is a positive. But I’m not sure it offsets their revenue growth engine effectively grinding to a halt.

Margins have continued to fall slightly quarter over quarter, with the last quarter showing a further slump in GAAP gross and FCF margins especially.

So what should we make of Align now we have full 2025 earnings to review? Firstly, the company continues to be profitable, showing stability in both their aligners and digital technology segments despite a cyclical downswing in the market. This means that any market upswing should have a net positive impact on its revenue, EPS, FCF and margins, which would likely lead to an earnings rerating and share-price gains for investors. When we look under the hood of this company, we see strength, while the numbers imply the opposite in some cases. However, we also see signs that they may be losing market share in their most important market, clear aligners. Let’s take a closer look. There is a lot of nuance to highlight here.

The Fundamental Business

As I noted in my previous article, while Align has seen flat/low single-digit revenue growth over the last few years, this is not necessarily shared by all of their competitors. I compiled the following (updated) table below.

I should note that the market for orthodontic solutions is fragmented, but their main peers can be considered as follows: Solventum (SOLV), Henry Schein (HSIC), Dentsply Sirona (XRAY), Envista Holdings (NVST), AngelAlign (AGLFF), and Straumann Group (SAUHY). Some of these companies also operate in other types of businesses with different verticals, so in order to make comparisons as relevant as possible I attempted to strip away the segments that are not comparable. I included relevant info/notes in these regards where I could, in the table below and also afterwards.

It is worth highlighting the following with regard to Align’s competitors:

  • Henry Schein management noted 'a decline in orthodontic sales' in both 2024 and 2025, suggesting that its revenue growth for the segment was partially offset by orthodontic revenue decline. Both Henry Schein and Dentsply Sirona are clearly losing market share in this segment.
  • Due to the Envista Holdings not separating out its revenue from this segment, it is too challenging to estimate this or their market share. However, Envista Holdings management highlighted increasing investment in its Spark brand aligners due to it being recognized as an area of prospective growth, noting: 'Our continued investment in Spark, our clear aligner system, has led to increased manufacturing capacity and continues to gain market adoption ... We believe that Spark will provide growth opportunities for our Orthodontic Solutions business.'
  • While it remains a small player in comparison to Align, AngelAlign has been rapidly gaining market share outside of mainland China. In 2024, 70% of its revenue was from the mainland, while in the first half of 2025 – the company has not reported full year 2025 revenue yet – this percentage dropped to 55.6%. Revenue from mainland China was flat for the 1H 2025 versus 2024, but outside of mainland China, revenue increased by a whopping 123.4%. Shipments increased by 103.5%. In time, they could become a major competitor, but that wouldn’t happen for a few years yet in my view. Still, they are one to watch, especially in terms of their efforts to penetrate the market outside of China.

We can see that Align’s revenue growth has slowed over the TTM period, while some of their competitors (Solventum, Envista, and AngelAlign) have seen revenue increases. Also, Straumann recognized stronger growth over the past year than Align. This would suggest declining market share.

Align’s falling gross margin (a year-on-year fall of almost 480 basis points, from 70.03% down to 65.26%) therefore highlights some vulnerability here. However, management noted that most of this was due to non-cash items in Q3 2025: restructuring charges, asset write-downs, depreciation and inventory write-offs. Outside of this, their non-GAAP gross margin for the year was 70.8%, up 10 basis points from the prior year.

Positively, the company posted higher volume shipments over the year, which is suggestive of sustained growth in their underlying business and broader market demand, as seen below.

While the company posted a 4.7% volume growth in terms of aligner shipments, year over year (as seen above), management noted of its gross margin decline in the most recent earnings call as follows, separating out aligners and imaging systems/service segments:

‘Clear Aligner gross margin for the fourth quarter was 64.2%, down 0.7 points sequentially primarily due to depreciation expense on assets disposed of other than by sale, partially offset by operational efficiencies … Clear Aligner gross margin for the fourth quarter was down 6 points year-over-year, primarily due to the depreciation expense of assets disposed of, other than by sale and lower ASP, partially offset by operational efficiencies … Systems and Services gross margin for the fourth quarter was 69.6%, up 8.4 points sequentially primarily due to excess inventory write-off in the third quarter, partially offset by lower ASP … Systems and Services gross margin for the fourth quarter was up 0.2 points year-over-year, primarily due to operational efficiencies partially offset by lower ASP.’

Management noted that foreign exchange was actually a favourable impact on their gross margins for both aligners and imaging systems/services (helping by 0.5% and 0.4% year over year, respectively), which implies their gross margins would have declined even further without this assistance. The lower ASPs (average selling prices) for both their aligners and digital technologies (software/hardware) suggests they might lack the pricing power I had previously assumed due to their market dominance, but some of this may be macro-related as I’ll discuss below.

In terms of market share, we can look to Straumann’s estimations, as seen in their latest earnings presentation. For clear aligners, they provide a figure for the total market size to be around CHF 4.9 billion (around $6.22 billion). If their company estimates are accurate, this would make Align’s market share in retainers around 52%, with their $3.25 billion 2025 revenue from this segment. This marks a 3% decline from 2024 figures, but perhaps more given currency fluctuations. From these figures alone, it would appear the company is losing market share, but is this just a temporary headwind or indicative of cracks appearing in their moat?

In terms of their imaging systems, Align generated just under $790 million for the full year. Straumann estimated at the end of 2025 the total value of this market at CHF 2.6 billion ($3.3 billion), but interestingly dropped this estimate to CHF 1.8 billion ($2.28 billion) when reporting their full year results 3 months later. SkyQuest estimated the market size as $2.94 billion for comparison.

Using their old Straumann figures for better comparison, this would give Align a 23.9% market share, representing the exact same figure from 2024. Using their new figures, this would give Align close to a 35% market share. In any case, we should use Stramann’s estimates here with caution, as they merely represent one single company’s figures.

While their iTero scanner and CADNAM software systems help strengthen their flywheel for customers, their competitors also access to have similar technologies. Envista have their own DEXIS IOS scanner among other digital scanning solutions, while Straumann have the SIRIOS intraoral 3D scanner. Henry Schein also has several scanners that it sells/distributes, while AngelAlign recently formed a partnership with DEXIS among other companies for access to their scanner technologies.

As such, I would query the extent to which we might call Align’s competitive positioning as unassailable in this space, given the barriers to entry for their competitors does not seem particularly high. Conversely, what may work to their advantage however are the high switching costs that company’s already using their technology and systems would be burdened with, but with a large global market that remains unpenetrated this is less of an advantage as it is in fully penetrated markets like North America. That is why their international expansion plans must remain a key focus for the company. Any suggestion that they are losing out to their competitors in external markets would be punishable by shareholders, who have likely already to some extent priced in their future dominance in new markets.

The Broader Industry

Align and their competitors are set to enjoy rising revenues due to secular industry growth. Several market research firms have offered their estimates, which I have updated from my previous coverage.

Back in August, the reports suggested an average of 15.9% but this has been updated significantly higher. It is unclear why - and I would take all of these estimates with a pinch of salt - but in any case I suggest that the main market/industry dynamics that I wrote about in my initial coverage remain, that is:

  • Aligners offer several advantages over traditional braces, including:
    • Less painful and more comfortable
    • Quicker results (for some treatments)
    • Fewer visits to the orthodontist
    • Easier to eat, brush and floss
    • Aesthetics/appearance (less unsightly)
  • Rising number of cases per doctor, suggesting increasing prevalence, alongside strong coverage by insurers. Funding for orthodontic treatments has become an industry standard in most employment benefits packages over recent years, and an increasing amount of this funding is being diverted from traditional braces to clear aligners.
  • Sustained global incidence of malocclusion, a misaligned bite (56% globally according to this study). Align management gives the figure of 75% prevalence. This suggests a very strong and persistent market demand for their products.
  • Enormous opportunity for international growth, outside of the US. Indeed, it is outside of North America where Align must focus their efforts if they have any hopes of returning a decent return to shareholders. Latin American and Asia represent the best growth opportunities in this market.

While Align has seen ASPs decline thereby impacting gross margins, this has been shown to be closely correlated with broader macroeconomic developments, according to a recent report from Orthodontics Products. They highlighted the economic elasticity of orthodontic treatment, comparing it to a luxury item. However, for many people it is less a luxury that they can do without entirely (like a new Gucci bag), but rather a luxury that they can afford to put off temporarily but will eventually feel the need to purchase. This makes the industry and Align’s revenues, specifically, subject to cyclicality.

As such, I would foresee growth rates to rise over the coming years as the people that would have put off orthodontic treatments over the past few years are now reaching the point where they can put them off no longer. We are likely to see this revenue ramp over the next few years, but only if the general economy improves would be my guess. I believe the market is slowly reaching a kind of inflection point, similar to the automobile industry in the past, where sales cycles can last several years or even a decade.

Outside of the US, management noted strong growth in Latin America, EMEA and APAC regions, all of which saw double-digit volume growth, whereas North America remained characterized by ‘continued softness’. Revenue from the Americas declined as a proportion of total revenue (from 49% to 47% in Q4 2025), which, given the strong growth in Latin America, suggests much of the decline was in the US and Canada. It is possible that we are reaching a point of saturation in the North American market, but we are still a long way from this point in other markets. As such, I predict that the marked improvements seen globally will help offset any declines closer to home. That said, just 2 points of international growth is hardly anything to write home about. If international represented closer to 60 or 70%, and growth rates there remained in the double digits per year, then we might be inclined to rerate Align much higher, which would be of massive benefit to shareholders. However, we are far from that.

In a broader sense and in terms of international growth potential, other regions are playing catch up to North America in terms of orthodontics, as seen in basic marketing trends. The company measured website visits and social media impressions, which is a strong indication of general interest in these types of treatments. They highlighted very strong penetration in the APAC (Asian Pacific) region especially.

Align is set to be a clear beneficiary of the general increase in product (and brand) awareness overseas. In especially the North American and European markets where it has a much stronger presence, the company has introduced some new products. Management remarked of their growth:

‘From a product standpoint, Invisalign First, the Invisalign palate expander and MAOB, Mandibular Advancement with Occlusal Blocks, continued to fuel year-over-year growth across all regions. Invisalign First is used for patients ages 6 to 10, addressing Phase 1 needs such as crowding, spacing, narrow arches and erupting teeth. Our palate expander system, the first direct printed orthodontic appliance and the only FDA-cleared removable palate expander remains a strong driver of early intervention adoption globally.’

Taken together, there are some clear risks and opportunities here. What investors will need to do is parse through it and assess the general impact of both.

It is possible that the present market softness in North America is a temporary thing. However, the question is just how temporary. Given the current economy, it is possible – if not likely – that many people continue holding back on expensive orthodontic treatments for themselves or their families until they can gain better sightlines into the next few years (jobs growth, recession risk, inflation, interest rates, etc.). So while I foresee further fluctuations in the share price here, I also suspect that over the long-term, Align will represent a solid investment. I do believe the period of double-digit growth in North America is behind us, and may never return, but for the next decade, we may see this being offset by sustained double-digit growth in other regions internationally. Providing shareholders can grab shares at a reasonable valuation, I see plenty of upside here. Let’s look at the company’s financials and valuation metrics to gauge how much future growth is already priced in and how much the recent 30% share price improvement since my last article has altered the risk-reward profile of the stock.

Financials and Valuation Metrics

Align Technology has cash and equivalents of $1.095 billion amid total assets valued at $6.234 billion. Goodwill represents $492 million of that, so not a proportion that would meaningfully skew its balance sheet unfavourably. The company has total liabilities valued at $2.185 billion and total debt of just $82.5 million. This is very low and provides the company with net cash per share of $14.11 (about 7.8% of its total market price).

It is worth noting that inventory has fallen over the past three years and that shareholders equity has ticked up slightly.

Their current return on equity is 3.44%, and it is a positive sign that returns (on equity, assets and invested capital) have remained positive throughout the downcycle.

In all, management seem excellent custodians of capital and the company has a solid balance sheet. Given they generated nearly $491 million in FCF over the TTM period – easily covering their total debt load – this would suggest they are in excellent financial health.

In terms of valuation, the recent uptick in share price has pushed the current ratios higher, but they are still at the low end historically. In truth, the company needed to be rerated once revenue growth slowed four years ago. With a current PE of 30 (using figures from Seeking Alpha), there may be more downside risk here, especially in the current market.

While a forward PE of 15 is not demanding for what some might still – at a push – consider a growth company with a decent moat, this definition (the growth part) should be put to the test over the next few quarters. Management guided for just 3-4% revenue growth throughout 2026 and mid single-digit volume growth in terms of product sales. Is this enough to buoy their share-price in the current, punishing market?

Slow North American growth will continue to hamper the company’s broader market penetration efforts in my opinion. Even when adding in a decent shareholder yield due to ongoing stock buybacks, shareholders will have to show a lot of trust and faith in the company’s growth projections to equate shares today with being cheap in my opinion, at least in terms of PEG ratio. This was a lot easier when they were priced below $150, but now investors might feel this is a good time to take some profits.

Management noted in the latest call that as of the Dec 31, 2025, the company has $831.2 million remaining available for common stock repurchases, under their $1 billion stock repurchase program announced in April 2025. The full utilization of this program would result in a 6.4% reduction in their OSC as judged by their current share price (around $180 per share). This would be a meaningful benefit to shareholders if they can manage this, but there is no guarantee they will. Beyond this, there are some important risks based on its valuation.

Concluding Thoughts

While they have a very strong market position in aligners and the software/hardware that supports their utilization, there are signs they may be losing market share. There is broad softness in their leading market region, which has slowly become increasingly saturated with their installed base of scanners. Ongoing market demand from patients for their aligners will sustain revenue, but not necessarily lead to much revenue growth in my opinion. Operational improvements here will only yield small percentage gains in terms of operating and net margins, but I expect these to remain slightly compressed as the company navigates its way through a troubling macroeconomic backdrop. Many customers will likely continue to hold off on orthodontic work for the time being, but in time I think this is likely to rebound like a coiled spring, with pent-up demand. However, there are no signs of this happening anytime soon. For now, there may be better investments out there for those seeking better growth prospects over the next 12 months or so.

Align Technology used to be considered a growth company, but it has been rerated for slow/no growth, and rightly so. Strong FCF and buybacks will provide some stability to its share price, especially in this environment when a company like this could, as such, be considered a beneficiary in a “flight to safety”. However, I perceive Align to be fairly valued now and therefore not the screaming buy it was at the end of last summer. Their recent positive share-price performance has taken much of the benefit out of “buying the dip” from the market reaction to their Q3 earnings call.

Shareholders who followed my advice back in August have done well here, but I urge caution as the broader market looks dicey and the company has not shown a meaningful path back to double-digit growth in its core North American market. Without this, I fear this company could be more permanently rerated as a stalwart. The company's shares could retest $200 but they could just as easily fall back to below $150 in my opinion. I am downgrading the stock to a hold, and would urge investors – current and prospective – to look toward international growth as the main driver going forward.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XPeng: Looks Like A Tesla That Keeps Its Promises, Long-Term Strong Buy
data.description:
In my quest to find value equities in the Chinese market, I continued my ongoing analysis of Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) today with an upgrade to Strong Buy. Those familiar with my work here will know that I have been bullish on Chinese equities since my second article on Seeking Alpha on January 22, 2025: FXI: China - The Ideal Hedge For A U.S. Equities Portfolio.

XPEV and TSLA - Starkly Similar But Very Different, Too

I turn my attention now to an EV powerhouse that is figuratively and literally driven by AI: XPeng Inc. (XPEV). In many ways, XPEV mirrors Tesla's (TSLA) growth when it was a young and rapidly rising threat to the conventional ICE industry with its mass-production EVs. The difference is that XPEV could eventually become the TSLA that delivers on its promises for a robotaxi and a humanoid robot. TSLA itself has fallen down on those promises, and I continue to rate it a Strong Sell at 16 percent lower than when I wrote the last article earlier this year.

Other similarities include a vision for a superior EV charging experience across its markets and developing a full-stack offering from in-house AI silicon to native AI infrastructure, as well as the foundation models required for its key focus area: Physical AI.

Similarities notwithstanding, XPEV does not have the executional hurdles that a company like TSLA does. TSLA has all the things XPEV does, but it seems to be going nowhere fast. Its Chinese counterpart, on the other hand, is executing a long-term strategic goal in successful tranches, and one sees that continuity in its earnings calls every quarter.

For instance, Q1 2025 announced the launch of the Mona M03 Max, Q2 reported that the Max trim was "over 80% of total MONA M03 sales", Q3 noted that the MONA M03 was a "leading product", and Q4 established its position as "the best-selling battery electric sedan in the RMB 100,000 to RMB 200,000 segment." I love the continuity of the ongoing narrative.

Another example - one that is equally, if not more, crucial to its development of physical AI - is its Turing AI SoC, which progressed along the following path:

At Q1 2025:

2025 signifies the launch of XPeng's full-scale implementation of the physical world foundation model in AI-powered vehicles

At Q2:

In July, we launched the G7, our first model featuring in-house developed a Turing AI SoC. Over the past 4 weeks, it has become the top among its competitors with the Ultra trim making up over 50% of sales.

At Q3:

XPeng's Turing AI SoC has earned a formal sourcing designation from Volkswagen with codeveloped vehicles expected to start mass production early next year.

[Author's note: This was reported on the Q3 call, but the nomination was secured on November 5, 2025, so technically Q4)]

At Q4:

Since entering mass production and being deployed in vehicles in Q3 last year, the XPeng Turing SoC has actually shipped over 200,000 units. Starting from the second quarter of this year, all XPeng models, including the Max trims which will fully transition to our in-house touring SoC shipment to our SoC.

Shipments of Turing SoC are targeted to reach nearly 1 million units this year. The Turing SoC is positioned to become the leading high compute AI SoC deployed on the edge by shipment volume. Volkswagen is our first external customer for both our Turing SoC and VLA 2.0. Our deep collaboration with leading global automakers, together with our ability to mass produce efficiently demonstrates that our technology is scalable, replicable and globally competitive. We welcome more automakers embody AI companies and Tier 1 suppliers to adopt our Turing SoC and integrate intelligent solutions for their customers.

One cannot but be impressed by this type of precise execution that moves the needle significantly quarter after quarter on multiple fronts. This is what TSLA sorely lacks, in my view. XPEV has delivered on multiple milestones that TSLA is still fumbling with, including robotaxis, humanoid robots, and even a sub-$25,000 EV, about which Musk said this in November of 2024:

Basically, I think having a regular $25K model is pointless. It would be silly.

Incidentally, the Mona M03 Max was introduced not three months before Musk said that, and the top trim was priced at $21,850 at the time.

Tesla - Promises made but largely unkept or with unknowable timelines

XPeng - Promises made and iteratively delivered quarter after quarter.

Q4/FY 2025 Milestones

Also, unlike TSLA, XPEV keeps achieving new milestones while simultaneously setting new ones with specific timelines. While the Musk-led company struggles to keep extending its timelines, XPEV is busy extending EV range, particularly with the milestone announcement of mass production of the Kunpeng Super Extended Range EV X9.

Its partnership with Volkswagen also saw a significant milestone with the start of deliveries. The VLA 2.0 system has passed closed-venue tests and is already powering the GX Robotaxi in Level 4 road tests in China and, soon, overseas; and, as I quoted above, the Turing AI SoC has a hard target of 1 million shipments in 2026. Another promise I believe will be kept.

On the financial side, the company has been setting equally important milestones, not the least of which is Q4 being its first net-profit quarter. Net income was reported at RMB 0.38 billion, or slightly over $55 million. This does not look impressive at first sight, but the trajectory over the past three years certainly does. The chart below is on a TTM basis, so it still shows net losses, but XPEV has successfully set the tone for a profitable 2026, with revenue and profitability growing hand in hand.

IRON, its humanoid robot, is making a milestone of its own this year, with plans for mass production by year-end. The Turing AI SoC and VLA 2.0 play pivotal roles here, bringing IRON to market preparedness with fourth-gen motion control capabilities. The company has set a goal of 1,000 units produced monthly by the end of 2026.

Despite All This...

... the company's ADSs are trading 20 percent lower than a year ago. The current price of $17.68 after Friday's close is ludicrously close to its IPO issue price of $15 per ADS. Part of the reason was the dismal outlook projected by management for Q1 2026. Under a worst-case scenario, the company is guiding for a revenue decline of up to 22.84 percent and deliveries dropping by as much as 35.11 percent, both on a year-on-year basis.

Ironically, this is excellent for my thesis, because strong performers with temporary headwinds offer the unique proposition of a potential rerating to the upside once these winds blow their course. The average Chinese consumer is under tremendous pressure but equally resilient, as is the nation's economy. I believe XPEV may be guiding conservatively here, and the decline ranges "29.79% to 35.11%" for deliveries and "16.01% to 22.84%" in total revenues seem to have been quickly priced in by the market, with the stock down over 8 percent over Friday's trading.

At this level, valuation still remains challenging. Extrapolate the $55 million in net income for Q4 through the end of FY 2026, and you get a P/E of 76, approximately.

If one wants to be more precise, one could model the 35 percent decline expected for Q1 2026. XPEV delivered 94,000 vehicles in Q1 2025 (year-ago period), so we shall take the worst-case 35 percent decline guidance from management and calculate a more accurate forward P/E.

A 35 percent decline would bring that to about 61,100 deliveries expected in Q1 2026. Assuming a more simplistic accounting calculation with revenue recognition on delivery, XPEV's revenue from 116,249 vehicles in Q4 2025 was $2.73 billion. At 61.1k, it will be $1.44 billion. Total revenue less vehicle revenue is assumed at the same level ($3.18 billion less $2.73 billion) of $450 million (0.45 billion), which gives us an estimated Q1 2026 top line of $1.89 billion. Street analysts predict $1.9 billion, so my calculation is not far off.

On a revenue of $1.9 billion, and because COGS reductions have been significant in prior periods, we shall imply a 20 percent gross margin to arrive at a gross profit of $380 million.

Since much of XPEV's operating costs are tied to fixed R&D and SG&A costs, I shall discount the actuals as of Q4 2025 - $410 million and $400 million, respectively - because those are the only levers the company can pull at the operating level. I should back out other operating income of $120 million, as reported for Q4 2025, but since that was from an increase in subsidies, I chose not to.

This calculates to a net loss of $0.43 billion, or $430 million. We do not have any visibility further than Q1 2026 from the company, and the Street does not seem to have modeled it, either, which is why we see quarterly revenue expectations for Q2 2026 still at $3.02 billion. Either they have not modeled it, or they expect a sharp recovery in the second quarter and thereafter. The latter seems unlikely.

In my model, therefore, I project a steady-state $430 million net loss for all four quarters, which equates to $1.72 billion in net losses through FY 2026. With 1.9 billion shares outstanding (basic and diluted), that gives us a net loss per share of 90 cents.

The one analyst who projected for FY 2026 is clearly less pessimistic on earnings than I am, but several others are positive for a full year of profits in FY 2027, with a projected forward P/E (non-GAAP EPADS) of 25. I think it may even happen in FY 2026 because XPEV will do everything in its power to ensure a positive bottom line. The milestone set in Q4 is now one that they must attempt to surpass quarter-on-quarter. One must bear in mind the operational levers in XPEV's hands, and that my projection for FY 2026 is an extremely conservative one.

Fortunately, XPEV's financial standing leaves nothing to be desired. Although the company was a serial diluter in the past, that is no longer a problem.

On its balance sheet, XPEV reported a "cash position of RMB47.66 billion (US$6.81 billion)", and at the earnings call, the company reported a "free cash flow -- inflow of approximately RMB 5 billion in 2025", so I see no reason to worry about top- or bottom-line trends reversing to the downside for as long as the headwinds last. Even if they last a whole fiscal year, as I have modeled, XPEV has a 'survival buffer' to withstand such a prolonged downturn.

On a price-to-sales basis, XPEV looks very attractive at a forward multiple of 1.29 as of this writing. The only reason that the declines projected for Q1 2026 look abysmal is that they compare to a quarter that boasted a 140-percent growth rate. Q2 should be a little less challenging to comp, but still a tough nut to crack.

I cannot predict the short-term trajectory for XPEV because the present is uncertain. I can, however, predict with greater confidence that EVs and hybrids will feature strongly in a prolonged high oil price environment, particularly in non-China markets like Europe, where the oil shock has hit hard.

China, for its part, is already in dialogue with Iran about securing safe passage through Hormuz for China-destined ships, and some have reportedly made it through. The positive impact on EV sales within China is likely to be marginal from this catalyst, but XPEV is already ramping its overseas sales and plans to hit 20 percent in non-China revenues in FY 2026. Sales and service stores outside China are targeted to double to 680 through the end of the year, and its ultrafast charging network is also expected to see rapid growth as XPEV expands into 10 outside markets this year.

All of this tells me there is tremendous value building up in XPEV, which I think justifies a Strong Buy at this time, with the caveat that we could see continued downward pressure on the stock. Therefore, although my long-term view is extremely bullish, I would recommend DCA-ing into a weighting that you are comfortable with - a standard 3 percent to 5 percent would be ideal, as that would give one a good balance between a) meaningful portfolio impact from incoming catalysts (headwinds abating) and b) diversification to spread the risk (in the event of a prolonged pullback on depressed revenue and profit).

data.title: Vistra: Nuclear Deals And AI Demand Support Growth; Risks Remain
data.description:
Vistra Corp. (VST) has had a messy year. A lot has changed: operations, rules, and how they spend money, all shifting around fast. The stock has dropped ~15.5% since the last “Hold” call at $179; now it’s down at $151.02. That’s a big slide from the late-2025 high of $219.82. Most of that is just the market cooling off after everyone got excited and pushed the price up too quickly. Now, there’s a bit more clarity on what the company will actually earn in the long run and how much cash they’ll hand back to shareholders, but Wall Street hasn’t totally caught up to that yet. There was a recent short-term bounce following headlines about Nvidia and Emerald teaming up with power firms, but it doesn’t really settle whether Vistra is building real, lasting value or just caught up in hype.

Earnings Growth Outlook

The main reason people stay bullish on Vistra comes down to actual numbers. They’re set up for a real jump in earnings over the next few years, plus a bigger chunk of their cash flow will be predictable and locked in. By 2026, revenue estimates hit $22.7 billion and then $24.3 billion in 2027. Earnings per share should move from $8.63 up to $11.22 over that time. That’s partly because Vistra’s all over different markets, so they get some leverage, and also because they’ve completed deals like Energy Harbor and Lotus and announced the pending acquisition of Cogentrix, expected to close in 2026. The company said adjusted EBITDA for 2026 should land at levels meaningfully above the $5.9 billion achieved in 2025, and more than 60% of that should turn into adjusted free cash flow before growth. In 2025 specifically, Vistra generated approximately $5.9 billion of adjusted EBITDA and $3.6 billion of adjusted free cash flow before growth, a conversion rate of roughly 61%. What’s changed lately is that more of their earnings are tied down by contracts: almost half the 2026 EBITDA will come from retail and PPA deals. That’s a big jump compared to past years, and it makes the whole business steadier and better protected if things go wrong and gives them more room to either return cash or put it back into new projects.

Meta and Amazon have locked in long-term nuclear power deals with Vistra, twenty-year contracts promising mid-to-high teen returns. That’s a big shift for Vistra’s core business. Management has stated that upon achieving full ramp-up of all nuclear agreements, there is a pathway to nearly 25% adjusted free cash flow before growth accretion on an annual basis. These contracts pretty much lock in steady cash flows well into the 2030s.

Cogentrix Acquisition

The Cogentrix deal should close in the second half of 2026. That’ll bring in about 5,500 megawatts of efficient gas generation at a price that works out to about $730 per kilowatt of capacity, and management expects the transaction to deliver mid-single-digit adjusted free cash flow before growth per share accretion in 2027, with high single-digit accretion on average over 2027 to 2029. Vistra says this will push free cash flow up by a mid-to-high single-digit percent in the back half of the decade. When this closes, Vistra’s gas fleet jumps to 26 gigawatts. That’s a lot of capacity, and it puts them in a good spot to grab more of the AI and data center power demand that just keeps growing.

They’re planning to allocate about $3 billion to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends in 2026 and 2027, and they currently have approximately $1.8 billion of share repurchase authorization remaining. After spending roughly $3 billion on shareholder returns and about $4 billion on growth investments, including the Cogentrix acquisition, the Permian gas units, and PJM nuclear uprates, Vistra still expects to have more than $3 billion in additional capital available to allocate through 2027. They want to get their net debt down to about 2.3 times adjusted EBITDA by year-end 2027. Management keeps saying they’re “highly hedged” over the next few years. They also say that if they hit these targets—lower debt and more predictable earnings from contracts—it could lead to credit rating upgrades, maybe even before the year’s out.

Power Demand Trends

Vistra’s market position keeps getting stronger. Big jumps in power demand from AI and giant data centers—this isn’t hype, it’s happening. ERCOT and PJM continue to see structurally improving demand, with management noting U.S. electricity consumption reached an all-time peak of approximately 4,200 terawatt hours in 2025, up about 2.5% year over year. Management has also emphasized that while data center demand is real and significant, the most meaningful impact on supply-demand dynamics may not begin until late 2027 or early 2028. That’s the real business base for Vistra’s margins to grow. Whenever people start saying “AI-driven demand isn’t real,” Vistra can point to its 20-year deals with tech companies already contracted. They also have a path to contract up to an additional 3.2 gigawatts at Beaver Valley and Comanche Peak, so there’s a lot of future growth potential locked in.

But just having strong sales doesn’t erase all the risks. Getting things done on the ground is still tricky. Fires at Martin Lake and Moss Landing have caused real outages, and running a big, growing fleet of plants isn’t simple. Mistakes while taking over Cogentrix or trying to boost output further aren’t small issues, especially with all the moving parts between grid operators and regulators.

Valuation

The valuation now sits at about 16.9x expected 2026 earnings and roughly 10x forward EV/EBITDA, plus a 9.3 price-to-cash flow on forward estimates. At today’s price, these numbers indicate that investors are already counting on Vistra to deliver, getting capital allocation right, making the Cogentrix deal work, and ramping up projects without a hitch. All the main growth options are already factored in. So the way things are set up now, most of the potential is only there if the company beats already tough expectations.

Shares dropped from $179 to $151.29, down almost 16% since the “Hold” call. That quick pop after AI-related headlines mostly looks like traders piling into another growth narrative, not necessarily a big change to the company’s core financial outlook. Most folks aren’t getting excited about earnings until the company actually posts steady, through-the-cycle EBITDA and shows free cash flow from PPA deals really happening.

Hold Rating Maintained

Management still talks up the growth angle: “adjusted free cash flow before growth per share to exceed $12.50 for 2026” and “increase to approximately $16” with buybacks and new deals, but they’re clear: any extra lift from new contracts or unused cash is a bonus, not the base plan. If there’s a big plant shutdown, a delayed project, or a surprise from regulators, the whole story falls apart. Any of these, especially if earnings miss, will make balance sheet issues a lot more obvious.

I’m sticking with Hold because the stock isn’t cheap and there’s still a lot that can go wrong. Sure, there’s a shot at real upside, especially if hedging and new contracts show up as bigger cash flow, but nothing here looks wildly mispriced. I’m waiting to see if they can keep EBITDA moving and actually turn more of it into free cash flow, quarter after quarter. Until that happens, it makes sense to stick with Hold, maybe picking up shares if the price drops or once there’s clear proof they’re actually delivering.

data.title: DICK'S Sporting Goods: Strong Outlook Ahead, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Sporting goods retailer DICK'S Sporting Goods, Inc. (DKS), is performing strongly in the face of a challenging consumer environment.

The company recently beat expectations on its Q4 results, and the retailer is also likely to benefit from a number of forward tailwinds. This includes a growing market for youth sports and World Cup-related spending.

Shares are down about 6% over the past month and are also down 4% YTD.

With shares trading near the back half of its 52-week range, I believe the company is worth additional attention. While I like the valuation at current levels, I believe the volatility in the current overall markets could create a better entry point for new investors seeking positioning in the stock.

DKS Stock Key Metrics

Shares in DKS currently command a forward trading multiple of about 13x earnings. This compares favorably to the broader markets, and though the multiple is slightly above both the historical and sector averages, it’s not significantly so.

The valuation grading is poor on Seeking Alpha’s (“SA”) quant metrics, but I don’t necessarily agree with this.

For example, the DKS’s enterprise value is just 10x forward EBITDA. That’s one click above both the sector and historical averages. However, the quants view that as a ‘C’ score. I would provide a higher rating here.

DKS also grades negatively on other metrics, and this is resulting in DKS being rated as an overall ‘hold’ by the quants. Sentiment is similarly neutral among the SA community, with one recent article even pointing to shares as a ‘sell.’

For Wall Street’s part, the community is highly bullish, with most seeing upside potential of at least 20% in the stock. This would take DKS to the $230/share mark, right in line with the 52-week highs.

I view this as attainable, though I do believe today’s economic environment could create some near-term headwinds in terms of consumer discretionary spending.

DKS Operating Performance

In my view, DKS’s Q4 was the topping on the cake for what was already a very solid year operationally heading into the print.

In Q4, comps increased 3.1%, building on a tough 6.6% comparison last year and bringing the two-year to nearly 10%. I view that as a pretty clear signal that demand remains healthy, even in a more pressured consumer environment.

In addition, growth was driven by both higher average tickets and continued engagement, with more athletes shopping and spending more per visit.

Profitability was also better than expected. Gross margin expansion, for example, accelerated in the quarter, driven by merchandise margin gains, while operating margin came in around 11%.

On the bottom line, non-GAAP EPS of $4.05/share was up nearly 12% year-over-year. And for the full year, the company put out record sales, comps of 4.5%, and EPS of $14.58/share, above the high end of guidance.

While the Foot Locker acquisition weighed on consolidated margins, that was largely expected.

The company also continues to gain market share, supported by its differentiated assortment and omnichannel execution.

To me, it was also good to see that overall performance was broad-based across key categories like footwear, apparel, and hardlines. This was a sign to me that this wasn’t a one-off driven by a single segment.

What Is The Outlook For DKS Stock?

Looking ahead, I believe DKS entered 2026 with a solid amount of momentum, and I believe the management team’s guidance reflects a continuation of that trend.

In laying out forward guidance during Q4, CFO Navdeep Gupta guided for comp sales growth in the range of 2% to 4%. At the midpoint, this implies a healthy degree of continued market share gains, in my view.

Operating margins are also expected to hold around 11%, with modest expansion potential as the year progresses.

As I noted in my introduction, I believe there are several tangible tailwinds supporting this outlook. First, the youth sports market remains a major driver, in my view, with families now spending over +$40B annually on kids’ sports.

DICK’S has firmly positioned itself at the center of this ecosystem, benefiting from both higher participation and increasing spend per athlete. I view this as a durable growth driver that should continue to support comps and customer loyalty.

On the more near-term side, catalysts like tax refund season and the upcoming World Cup are expected to provide incremental demand, as these events tend to drive traffic and basket size, particularly across key categories like footwear and apparel.

While there may be some near-term earnings pressure tied to investments and the Foot Locker integration, the broader outlook looks positive through my lens.

Is DKS Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

All considered, I view DKS’s business in a positive light, but I am neutral on the stock at current levels.

In my view, DKS is performing strongly, with consistent comp growth, strong margins, and clear market share gains. The company also has a number of favorable tailwinds, including its dominant positioning in youth sports and incremental demand drivers like the World Cup and tax refund season.

I do believe, however, that much of this strength is already reflected in the current valuation. At roughly 13x forward earnings, shares are not expensive. However, they may be priced just right, especially when considering the uncertain macro environment and potential pressure on discretionary spending.

Additionally, while the Foot Locker integration presents upside in the longer term, it does introduce some near-term uncertainty and possible earnings variability.

From my perspective, I do see a path to upside, particularly if the company continues to top expectations and macro conditions remain stable. However, I also think volatility in the broader markets could present more attractive entry points. That said, I view DKS as a 'hold' at current levels.

data.title: AMD May Have More Upside Than Nvidia (Upgrade)
data.description:
As the rotation out of tech names gained momentum at the start of the year, I downgraded my rating on Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) earlier this year. I'm glad I did so, because the stock was pretty much flat since my last coverage.

I now upgrade back to a strong buy, as I see strong signs that the rotation out of tech is reversing its course. Leaving the macro risks on the side (a topic I discussed in this coverage), I think AMD could see a strong second half of the year.

In fact, I argue that the upside is higher for AMD than for Nvidia, at least from a price action perspective.

In this piece, I discuss my view on the main pillar of the bull case on AMD: its pivot from selling individual components to rack-scale systems.

There is still a long uphill road ahead until AMD takes market share from Nvidia. However, to me, it's a matter of when, not if. In my view, the "when" is likely the back half of the year, with Q4 more likely than Q3.

I upgrade to a strong buy, and I expect to see new highs before the end of the year.

From Accelerators to Rack-Scale Systems

The title above represents what I believe to be the main pillar of the bull case on AMD.

The company is pivoting from selling individual components like the Instinct MI-3xx/-4xx series GPUs and the EPYC series CPUs to a full-stack offering. In fact, this pivot is the specific reason they acquired ZT Systems for $4.9B last year.

This is exactly what the incumbent leader, Nvidia, is doing with its GB300 NVL72 rack-scale system. On top of selling the Blackwell GPUs (there are 72x GPUs in the NVL72 configuration) and the Grace CPUs (36x), the company is also offering interconnect, networking, systems, and software.

Let me put into perspective now how important these ancillary products are in the AI data center buildout. In its last fiscal year (FY 2026), Nvidia reported $31.38B in data center networking revenue.

That's almost 2x AMD's data center revenue in FY 2025 ($16.6B).

So, networking alone is bringing Nvidia almost twice as much revenue as AMD's total data center segment. In fact, I strongly believe that Nvidia's turnkey full rack-scale AI systems are what made it gain market share in the GPU space so quickly.

It's not that people want to buy GPUs alone. They buy them via rack-scale systems, and there are 72x in each NVL72 setup. That's why Nvidia has a 90% share in the data center compute market.

How did I come up with that figure?

AMD's Q4 2025 data center revenue was $5.4B. NVIDIA's data center revenue in Q4 FY26 was $51.3B. That's a total market of $56.7B. As a side note, Broadcom doesn't make GPUs, so I'm not counting its ASIC revenue here. As for Intel, well, I'm going to leave Intel on the sidelines, given that its Gaudi 3 data center GPU is not widely used by hyperscalers and its next-gen GPU, Crescent Island, is not expected until H2 2026 (customer sampling, not ramp).

Back to AMD, its data center market share is barely in the low teens. Therefore, I was very pleased when AMD showcased the Helios rack-scale system in October last year. Helios integrates Instinct GPUs, EPYC CPUs, and Pensando networking. As for the software, Helios is designed to work with the ROCm, which has a limited adoption when compared to Nvidia's CUDA software stack.

That said, AMD's software stack didn't deter the likes of OpenAI, Meta, or Oracle. The 6 GW OpenAI deal, worth tens of billions of dollars in annual revenue, was signed in October 2025 and requires the first 1 GW of capacity to be deployed starting in H2 2026. The other 6 GW deal with Meta contains a similar timeline, with the first GW deployment expected to begin in H2 2026.

The last important one is the expansion with Oracle, which includes 50,000 AMD Instinct MI450 GPUs starting in Q3 2026.

Well, there is actually a fourth one, after the company expanded its collaboration with Tata Consultancy Services in February this year, for 200 MW.

What I find encouraging is the customer traction as soon as the company announced its Helios rack-scale system.

Given the momentum in the AI data center buildout, I wouldn't be surprised to see more partnerships with the other hyperscalers this year. So what?

Unlike the case for Nvidia (whose GPUs are present in pretty much any serious data center in the U.S.), these potential partnerships are not priced in for AMD.

In my view, AMD is the underdog here, and this presents optionality if the company is able to steal market share from the incumbent. That's why, once the war and inflation fears are over, I feel more bullish on AMD than Nvidia.

Risks

As I've shown above, AMD's market share in the data center compute segment is barely in the low teens. Therefore, even though the pivot toward rack-scale systems is encouraging, there is a long uphill road ahead.

The latest update in the Celestica partnership is that Helios will be customer ready in late 2026. It's still in the second half of the year, although I would have preferred to see it in early H2 rather than late H2.

Why?

Because growth is not particularly exciting right now. Management guided Q1 2026 revenue to about $9.8 billion, which is down about 5% sequentially from the Q4 2025 level.

In fact, the data center strength in Q4 last year looks less impressive if you strip out the China contribution. Specifically, without $390 million of China MI308 sales, AMD's Q4 data center revenue would have been $4.99 billion, below the $5.07 billion analysts expected.

I warn readers that volatility remains on the table until the company is able to recognize sales for its Helios systems.

Looking Ahead

The stock is trading at 30x forward earnings. That's more expensive than Nvidia's 21x multiple or Broadcom's 27x forward P/E.

This is a compliment for AMD, by the way.

Unlike Nvidia, AMD's valuation didn't compress as much in the tech selloff since October last year. In fact, even after Huang's $1T upgrade (from $500B initially) in sales potential for Nvidia's Blackwell and Rubin AI chips through 2027, the stock has barely reacted. In other words, the market is complacent with Nvidia's outstanding results.

In my view, that's not the case for AMD, as noted by the high single-digit spike on February 24, after announcing the Meta deal.

Overall, I upgrade AMD to a strong buy, and I expect shares to retest new highs in the back half of the year.

data.title: Why Annaly Capital Is My Favorite 'End Of The World' Hedge
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving.

Sing with me:

"It's the end of the world as we know it. It's the end of the world as we know it. It's the end of the world as we know it. . . And I feel fine!" – R.E.M.

We often get wrapped up in what is happening in the world, and more specifically, in how the world is changing. Today's world is unrecognizable from the world my grandfather grew up in.

In World War II, it was an amazing technological advancement for people to be able to go to the movie theater to get news updates on the war. These were generalized updates covering the major events.

Fast-forward to today, and there are updates on every individual attack. We can pull up videos of almost everything that happens and even track the movements of warships at a level that WW2 generals could only dream about having in a classified briefing.

As with all technology, it comes with its positives and negatives. It's good to be informed and know what is going on; it is rather unhealthy to sit there refreshing your page every 15 minutes to read about the next back-and-forth.

Added to the speed at which information is relayed around the world today is the speed at which sometimes convincing videos can be created from nothing using AI. When you watch a video, you don't really know whether to believe your own eyes or not.

We walk around in a world where we can access an enormous amount of information from our phones, which are rarely out of arm's reach. Unfortunately, we are also in a world where the quality of that information is highly questionable.

Is it the "end of the world"? As we know it—yes. The world is changing, and with it comes turmoil in the markets. Yet as the world changes, there will be some things that will always be the same.

Some investors will be gamblers, looking to make quick flips based on being faster to react to news than others. We see the oil markets gyrating in response to every attempted strike on oil-producing assets. Other investors will be long-term investors who buy and hold through the turmoil, with the conviction that the investments they hold will produce a good long-term return.

Today, we want to take a look at an investment that has gone through several major changes in the world.

Annaly Capital Management – Yield 13.5%

Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY) is the oldest mortgage REIT that focuses on agency MBS. It pioneered the investment strategies that its peers have followed. What makes Agency MBS an interesting investment is its ability to outperform during recessions.

Here is a look at NLY before and after the dot-com bust:

Note that it was underperforming materially from IPO until 2001, then it skyrocketed during the recession from 2001 to 2004.

Similarly, it declined and underperformed from 2004 to 2006, but then rallied as recession risk increased and had strong results through the GFC:

As we sit here today, NLY has underperformed the past five years, but is starting its rally up, having bottomed in late 2023.

Despite its price only being up 18%, NLY's total return has kept pace with the S&P 500 as its dividend remains robust. We have not seen the crossing point where the S&P 500 declines and NLY runs ahead, but that possibility is becoming increasingly probable.

Outperforming During Recessions

Why does NLY outperform during recessions? Quite simply, because NLY is invested in an asset class that has minimal credit risk. Agency MBS are mortgage-backed securities that hold mortgages guaranteed by the "agencies" Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. If a borrower defaults, the agency buys back the mortgage at par. As a result, materially all the credit risk is carried by the agency, not by the investors in agency MBS.

Agency MBS tends to have prices that correlate strongly with US Treasuries. They are often used as an alternative to US Treasuries for institutions that have high liquidity demands, like banks or insurance companies.

By holding an asset class that takes on minimal credit risk, NLY tends to see its book value improve in times when investors are looking for safety.

That isn't to say NLY avoids all risk. NLY avoids credit risk, which is one type of risk. The type of risk that NLY takes on is interest rate risk.

Profiting From Interest Rate Spreads

NLY's strategy is to borrow short-term money and hold Agency MBS, which typically has an expected life of about seven years. While mortgages are 30-year obligations, they amortize, which means principal is paid down every month, and they can be prepaid at any time. As a result, borrowers frequently pay off a mortgage early by making larger payments, selling their house, or refinancing.

NLY borrows in the "repo" markets, which are typically 30-90 day borrowings that are secured by the agency MBS they hold. The interest rate is usually slightly higher than the Federal Reserve Target Rate.

So, NLY is borrowing short-term and holding long-term assets. It profits from the difference. As a result, NLY's historical trading price has been highly correlated with how wide the spread is between the 10-year Treasury and 2-year Treasury.

When the United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) is significantly higher than the United States 2-Year Bond Yield (US2Y), NLY's price tends to rise. When the 10-year is below the 2-year Treasury, NLY's price tends to bottom. NLY outperforms so much during recessions because that's a period where the Treasury curve tends to steepen, and it steepens because we see the Federal Reserve slash rates.

The flip side is that NLY will struggle the most when the Fed is hiking short-term rates and short-term rates are lower than long-term rates. This is usually during periods of economic expansion.

NLY has been keeping pace with the S&P 500 (SP500) the past three years, even though we haven't seen a recession, and even though the Fed has been cutting rates at a very slow pace. Simply the end of the Fed hiking cycle and the inversion of the yield curve have been enough for NLY to post strong numbers and see the start of the recovery. History tells us that it's just getting started, and if we see a recession, NLY is one of the few stocks capable of being truly countercyclical and rising while the rest of the market falls.

Conclusion

It is the "end of the world as we know it" for various reasons. In the 2000s, the world as we knew it was ending. Computers have revolutionized so much in our lives that today it is difficult for many of us to imagine living without them. Overall, most of us would consider that a positive, as we live a much higher standard of living, and many of us can trace our labor to the internet.

There were definitely some negatives as well. Technology can be used for good or bad. We use technology to heal the sick, while simultaneously using it to wage war on each other. AI will be the same. It will be used for great things and for bad things. There is simply no way around that.

As investors, we need investments that we know can be bedrocks through challenging periods—that will step up and outperform during periods that are difficult.

NLY will not be the best holding in your portfolio all the time. Their earnings and, therefore, their dividend will fluctuate with the interest rate curve. Yet, when recession strikes and uncertainty rises, it is one of those holdings that can really shine when fear is at its highest.

While others are singing about the end of the world, we will feel fine.

data.title: DiDi Global: Waiting For International Segment To Show Earnings Growth (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on DiDi Global Inc. (DIDIY) was a buy rating because all the growth levers were firing up, and I expected earnings growth to accelerate. Fast forward to today; I think the operating story is still intact, but the earnings story is not as clean anymore. Revenue and transactions were strong, and China continues to execute well. The issue is that International, while growing very quickly, saw losses widen sharply due to heavier reinvestment. That makes the timing of earnings acceleration harder to underwrite, which is why I am moving to a neutral view.

4Q25 results update

DIDIY reported a mixed quarter. While revenue was up 10.5% y/y, core platform transactions were up ~14% y/y, core platform GTV was up ~20% y/y, and platform sales from the China Mobility and International segments were up ~25% y/y, margins and profits were materially weaker. Cost of revenues increased 9.2% y/y, and operations and support expenses increased 15.5% y/y. The sales and marketing expense increase was the significant one, up 95% y/y. Other expenses like R&D were also up 17.3% y/y, and G&A expenses were up 27.7% y/y. Ultimately, the operating loss widened to RMB 2.7 billion, a big step up from RMB 0.2 billion.

China is still the core growth engine

I have no complaints about the China business; it is doing absolutely great. DIDIY managed to continue driving growth via better monetization and delivered higher segment profit. For reference, China Mobility has seen twelve consecutive quarters of double-digit growth, and in the latest quarter, average daily transactions reached a record 38.9 million. Coupled with the numbers I noted above, this is a pretty clean set of numbers. It is not as if DIDIY benefited from any one-off tailwind as well. Management said China GTV growth was mainly driven by more transactions, and platform sales growth was driven by GTV growth and improved operating efficiency. Then, they went on to say that adj. EBITA grew because of higher platform sales, albeit partly offset by higher consumer incentives. All of this indicates that DIDIY is benefiting from the flywheel effect of more rides, more platform revenue, and more profits.

Looking ahead, I would expect the China segment to continue compounding growth at the current rate. GTV probably continues to grow at high teens to low 20s percent, as management said it plans to keep investing in 2026 by raising driver engagement and offering more affordable product choices to consumers (this should drive up volume).

International is why the rating is neutral

It is also fair to say that International was the strongest growth driver for this quarter, but I think it is also the key reason why I downgraded to neutral. Here are some numbers for reference: transactions grew 24.5% y/y, driving GTV up 47.1% y/y, and that led to Platform Sales being up 22.3% y/y to RMB2.9 billion. Those are very strong top-line numbers, and since management said the GTV growth was mainly driven by higher transaction volume, demand is clearly there. But note that the segment adj. EBITA loss widened by a significant amount, up RMB2.7 billion y/y to RMB3.4 billion. This is not the type of organic growth that I like to see, and management confirmed that the wider loss was directly tied to higher spending on incentives and marketing. I have previously touched on the bull case: if DIDIY succeeds in pulling this off, it would have significant scale, and once it hits critical mass adoption, profits will start to flow in.

The good news is that management said international mobility demonstrated sustained profitability for the second consecutive year. He also said the recent step up in strategic investment in new overseas businesses in Q4 saw growth that exceeded expectations and that it was seeing synergies being realized across multiple business lines in Latin America. The problem is that management did not show how DiDi got from a profitable international mobility business to a reported international segment that still lost RMB3.4 billion in Q4. In simple terms, they did not show how much of the reported international segment loss came from the newer overseas businesses they are investing in and how much came from the core international mobility business itself (sustained profitability doesn’t mean profitability went up; it could have been diluted). I think this matters a lot for the equity story because if the core mobility business is truly profitable, then those losses may be more about growth investments, which investors can be more patient with if they believe scale will drive future earnings. But if profitability at the core is being diluted at the same time, then the earnings path is not that clean, and this uncertainty gives investors less reason to give management the benefit of the doubt.

For what it’s worth, I can see the upside case. Overseas growth is strong, management sounds confident, and they said that the underlying mobility operation is profitable. But the losses were real, and management did not frame them as a one-quarter issue that should quickly reverse (i.e., it could get worse). The spending was noted as a strategic investment, and management said it has plans to continue investing in 2026. Given the lack of certainty in the path to earnings growth accelerating, I think it’s best I sit on the sidelines for now.

Valuation

The earnings story is no longer as clean as I thought. At its core, the China growth story is still great, but the step up in reinvestment is likely to continue weighing on the near-term numbers. From a valuation perspective, this is very worrisome because it means the timeline for earnings growth to accelerate gets pushed out further. While revenue growth may accelerate, this is a question mark for now. Consensus has also cut its near-term estimates by a huge ton (as you can see from the chart above). The one-year forward PE multiple is no longer relevant given negative earnings, so the right metric is the two-year forward (more normalized), which is now at 13x. The problem with this is that it implies reinvestments stop, and DIDIY starts letting revenue flow through to earnings. Given I have no confidence in when this reinvestment cycle will stop, I am refraining from making a judgment on what multiple deserves to trade at today. For this reason, I downgrade to hold.

Conclusion

My revised view for DIDIY is now a hold rating. I still like the underlying demand story. China remains strong, and there is a credible upside case if the international scale eventually drives more profits. But I do think the recent Q4 quarter put a dent in the equity story because of the step-up in international losses, together with management’s plan to keep investing, which means the path to earnings growth is less visible than I previously thought. Until I get better clarity on that trade-off, I think staying on the sidelines is the right call.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nationwide Loomis Short Term Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Environment

The bond market delivered a solid total return with low volatility in the fourth quarter, closing out a generally positive year for the asset class. Fixed-income assets remained supported by a backdrop of slow but positive economic growth, an annualized inflation rate that largely held below 3%, and accommodative US Federal Reserve (Fed) policy. The Fed enacted two-quarter point interest rate cuts and announced the end of its multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Additionally, investors appeared to anticipate that the Fed was likely to continue easing in 2026. These developments, in combination, fueled positive returns across all major segments of the market.

Performance Review

The Fund underperformed its benchmark during the quarter, 1.06% (IS share class) vs. 1.16%. An overweight allocation to High Yield Corporates was additive to performance, as was Investment Grade Corporates. A US Treasury underweight was a drag on performance for the quarter.

Overall, strategy risks for the Fund declined during the quarter. Corporate bond risk declined slightly and remains near 50% of risk budget. While spreads widened during the quarter they remain narrow and the investment grade risk premium is also at the low end of the range. Lack of bottom-up opportunities heading into year-end provided the team room to reduce risk. Commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) risk continues to be allocated in the lower half of our risk budget. Asset-Backed Securities (ABS) risk remained steady, using about one-third of risk budget.

Outlook

Fixed income markets delivered a solid positive total return as rates declined while the curve steepened on the back of two 25 basis points (bps) Federal Reserve (Fed) cuts during the fourth quarter. Risk premiums, or credit spreads, drifted slightly higher but remained near historic tights. The Federal Reserve continues to grapple with their dual mandate as inflation remains elevated while labor market conditions have deteriorated. The lack of official data due to the extended government shutdown made policy decisions more challenging for the Federal Reserve and there was an increase in voting member dissents over the last two meetings. The Fed Funds Rate target now stands at 3.75%. Markets are currently pricing in only a 50% probability of another cut in the first quarter of 2026, although just over two more cuts are priced by year end 2026. Spread sectors remained well supported, with new supply easily absorbed.

Corporate fundamentals remain at a healthy level by historical standards. Growth in the first quarter of 2026 may receive a boost through higher tax refunds and a reversal of the negative impacts from the shutdown. However, weak corporate pricing power and rising debt service costs suggest margin compression going forward. On the consumer front, we believe that moderating demand for labor, the continued decline in excess savings, and increasing impatience with higher prices are potential catalysts for weaker spending, which may be further exacerbated by higher prices for goods.

We continue to believe that we are in the late expansion phase of the credit cycle, with growth slowing to below trend, while inflation - which is still above target, should moderate as the effects of tariffs dissipate. While we do not anticipate that the US will enter a recession, we continue to see elevated risk of a downturn scenario. We are concerned that slowing rates of hiring and reduced affordability may weigh more heavily on incomes and consumption. Additionally, the risk of additional Federal Government stimulus leading up to mid-term elections may also add to the growing fiscal burden. This concern has been evident in the still elevated level of term premium in the longer end of the yield curve. Market volatility may remain elevated due to ongoing geopolitical tensions and interventionism.

We continue to favor spread sectors, such as corporate bonds and securitized assets. The Fund's corporate bond risk relative to benchmark decreased slightly over the quarter but remains around 50% of risk budget. The team continues to hold a small number of high yield issuers we believe offer value.

We believe asset-backed securities (ABS) continue to be a favorable alternative to front end US Treasurys. We generally favor consumer-related collateral and prefer the top part of the capital stack but are not limited to the highest quality band of the capital structure. Currently, the Fund's ABS risk relative to benchmark is around one-third of risk budget.

The Fund continues to hold commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), with the current benchmark relative risk in the lower half of the risk budget. When opportunities arise within CMBS, we tend to favor senior parts of the capital stack. We continue to follow our process of seeking to build diversified portfolio while favoring asset classes that we believe can generate security selection.
